coredata.dc:title: Chemical exfoliation of pure graphene sheets from synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Son, Dong Ick
Kwon, Byoung Wook
Kim, Hong-Hee
Park, Dong Hee
Angadi, Basavaraj
Choi, Won Kook
coredata.dc:description: We report on a novel, simple and easy method to produce graphene layers using chemically synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell quantum dots. Initially, graphite powder was partially oxidized by acid treatment of graphite powder, which was then mixed with zinc acetate dihydrate in a dimethyl formamide solution. During this process, graphene sheets were detached from the graphite oxide layers through a layer-by-layer chemical peel-off and then partially covered from ZnO quantum dots which formed in the dimethyl formamide solution. Therefore, the ZnO–graphene quantum dots were dissolved in hydrochloric acid solution and then pure graphene layers were extracted from solution. The structural, optical, and electrical properties of obtained graphene layers were analyzed by using X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscope, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy instrument.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Induced functional groups on GO surface after treatment with an acid. (b) Embryo ZnO QDs formed under the chemical reaction and formation of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs. The folding of graphene layers outside the ZnO inner-core QDs comes from possible chemical reactions between three kinds of functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxy, and epoxy) on GO and Zn2+. (c) Schematics of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs. (d) Schematic of synthesized and unrolled graphene after removal of inner ZnO QDs by HCl. (e) Chemical process of the production of ZnO–graphene quasi QDs and graphene after removal of ZnO.

(a) XRD of pure graphene with (002) diffraction peak (top) and of the ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs including graphene and ZnO QDs (bottom). (b) Raman spectrum of the graphene layers obtained by dissolving ZnO from ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs (top) by HCl and ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs (bottom). The E 2 (high) phonon frequency is known to exist around 436cm−1 for ZnO nanocrystals [29].

(a) HRTEM image of ZnO–graphene quasi QDs revealing the outer graphene layers. (b) HRTEM image of ZnO QD covered by graphene (left). White dot circle is an image of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs. Red squared region is the magnified image of one ZnO QD covered by a monolayer graphene. An enlarged view of the monolayer graphene is indicated by red lines. The area in the white dotted square box is magnified to disclose the atoms more clearly: In the single layer, the grey colored atoms depict the hexagon and the hexagon center is black. (c) HRTEM image showing the graphene layer areas over the ZnO core. (d) High magnification TEM image shows the edges of graphene film consisting of one, two, three, four, and five layers. The cross-sectional view was achieved by the inherent characteristic curving of the film edge. In-plane lattice fringes suggest a local stacking order of the graphene layers. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) HAADF images of an area of sample with a varying number of graphene layers. (b) The spectrum of core-loss EELS of C K-edge over the region O1 in (a). (c) HAADF STEM images and enlarged HRTEM images of graphene films. The red circle line is the magnified image of graphene layers. From point 4 on the white square dot-line, an enlarged view of the five-layer graphene is indicated by a red dot-line. The area in the red dot-line framed boxes has been magnified to disclose the atoms more clearly. The C–C bond length in graphite is 0.14nm, and it is possible to see the graphene hexagons; three of those are marked in a honeycomb structure. In the HAADF images, atoms are seen as bright features, and these constitute the corners of the hexagon. The C–C bond length, although not clearly resolved everywhere in the patch, is measured to be 0.14nm, and the atom-to-atom distance across the hexagons, is 0.28nm. (d) Low energy EELS spectra of graphene films with N =1, N =3, N =6, and N =10 layers corresponding the positions of (c) showing the π- and the π+σ plasmon. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Chemical exfoliation of pure graphene sheets from synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Soft Innovative Materials Research Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Eunhari san 101, Bongdong-eup, Wanju-gun, Jeonbuk 565-905, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interface Control Research Center, Future Convergence Research Division, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Hwarangno 14 gil 5, Seoul 131-791, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Bangalore University, Bangalore 560 056, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on a novel, simple and easy method to produce graphene layers using chemically synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell quantum dots. Initially, graphite powder was partially oxidized by acid treatment of graphite powder, which was then mixed with zinc acetate dihydrate in a dimethyl formamide solution. During this process, graphene sheets were detached from the graphite oxide layers through a layer-by-layer chemical peel-off and then partially covered from ZnO quantum dots which formed in the dimethyl formamide solution. Therefore, the ZnO–graphene quantum dots were dissolved in hydrochloric acid solution and then pure graphene layers were extracted from solution. The structural, optical, and electrical properties of obtained graphene layers were analyzed by using X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscope, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy instrument.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, graphene has attracted tremendous attention and research interest, owing to its exceptional properties, such as high electronic conductivity, good thermal stability, and excellent mechanical strength [1–4]. Since the discovery of graphene, which is a flat monolayer of carbon atoms packed into a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice and a basic building block for graphitic materials, studies on its fabrication have generally been categorized into four different methods [5]. The first is the micromechanical exfoliation of graphite [1]. The second is chemical vapor deposition to grow large-area graphene films (up to about 1cm2) having a single to a few layers on metal substrates [6]. The third is epitaxial growth on electrically insulating surfaces such as SiC [7], and the fourth is chemically modified graphene (CMG) synthesis from colloidal suspensions made from graphite, derivatives of graphite oxide (GO), and graphite intercalation compounds [5,8]. At present, the CMG is usually achieved by using hydrazine [8,9], dimethylhydrazine [10], hydroquinone [11], NaBH4 [12,13], aerosol pyrolysis [14], solvothermal synthesis [15], Fe reduction [16], and reduction by using hydriodic acid with acetic acid (HI–AcOH) [17]. Recently, studies on the conjugated composites of graphene with oxide semiconductor nanocrystals through intermediary linking agents have been reported [2]. Using this method, pure graphene could be easily extracted from the graphene conjugated oxide semiconductor nanocrystals.

In this work, we demonstrate an easy technique for synthesizing graphene layers which were extracted from the chemically synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell structure quantum dots (QDs). After removal of the ZnO core by hydrochloric (HCl) acid, pure graphene layers with fewer defects were successfully obtained [2]. The obtained graphene layers were exhaustively characterized for their structural, optical and electrical properties using various techniques and equipment such as, structural property from X-ray diffraction (XRD) equipment, high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) technique, and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) technique, crystalline structure from Raman spectroscopy equipment, electronic structure from electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) technique and X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) equipment.

The chemical synthesis procedure of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell structure QDs is reported elsewhere [2] and briefly explained as follows. Graphite powder was ultrasonicated in a 3:1 mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 to get Graphite oxide (GO). After repeated washing with water by centrifugation, decantation and a final ethanol wash, the product was dried at 60°C for 1day. The GO material obtained through the acid treatment had a grayish color. Using dimethyl formamide (DMF) as a solvent, the GO was uniformly dispersed by ultrasonication. At the same time, zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in DMF, and added to the GO in DMF solution. The mixed solution was heated to 95°C and maintained at that temperature for 5h. Then, the appearance of the ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs changed to a white–grayish color. After washing with ethanol and water, the powder was allowed to dry. After drying, to exfoliate the graphene layers, the ZnO–graphene QDs were dissolved in HCl acid solution and then graphene layers were extracted from solution. The final product was washed with DI water and ethanol, and then dried in vacuum to obtain high quality graphene layers. This chemical synthesis process of graphene layers is graphically summarized in Fig. 1 (see also Supplementary information, Fig. S1). This chemical synthesis enables production of graphene in gram-scale quantities at practical reasonable shorter time.

Fig. 2 (a) presents the XRD patterns of chemically synthesized ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs (top) and chemically exfoliated graphene layers (bottom). The appearance of a broad intense peak G (002) at 2θ =25.8° and a broad low intense peak G (100) at 2θ =43.5° confirms the existence of graphene as shell and the intense peaks of ZnO (100), (002), (101), and (102) proves the existence of ZnO core in ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs2. However, the XRD pattern (bottom of Fig. 2(a)) of exfoliated graphene shows no peaks of ZnO but only a low intensity peak centered at around 2θ =26.5° related to graphene. This reveals that there is only the outer graphene shells remained while the ZnO core is being dissolved in HCl. The other evidences of structural and electrical properties of the graphene and ZnO–graphene QDs were presented in Supplementary S1-3, S1-4, S1-5, S3, and S5.

The optical properties of graphene sheets and the ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs were investigated through Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 2(b) presents the Raman spectra of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs (bottom) and chemically exfoliated graphene layers (top). In the bottom spectra of Fig. 2(b), the splitting of G band peak into two symmetric lines of G+ (1592.7cm−1) and G− (1566.6cm−1) is well evident and which could be well explained in terms of the induced strain due to the bending of graphene surrounding the ZnO QDs2. This splitting of G− and G+ is similar to that observed in carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [18]. The graphene covering the ZnO QDs with the position of the sharp 2D peak at ∼2692.4cm−1 was supported by a single sharp 2D peak below 2700cm−1 [2]. However, in the top spectra of Fig. 2(b), a broad D-band and a G-band without splitting were observed at 1355.6 and at 1577.5cm−1, respectively, and were in agreement with the reported peak positions of graphene [19]. The G peak for graphene sheets occurs at around 1580cm−1, and this peak for GO significantly shifts to higher frequencies due to graphite amorphization [20,21]. The position of 2D peak is known to be one of good parameters to determine the number of layers of graphene sheets [22,23]. Appearance of an asymmetric 2D peak detected at around 2684cm−1 means that the analyzed region consists of less than five layers of graphene. The graphene layers produced by chemical exfoliation are similar to those observed in graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) [24]. Also note that the Raman spectrum shows a strongly suppressed defect-related D-band peak in comparison with G-band peak, indicating overall good quality of the resulting graphene film due to transferring functional groups on GO surface to ZnO QDs during chemical exfoliation. The ratio of the I D/I G (=0.1) of graphene from graphite–ZnO synthesis is much lower than any values ever reported in previous chemical methods, such as through NaBH4 (>1) [13], solvothermal synthesis (1.16) [15], Fe reduction (0.32) [16], reduction by using hydriodic acid with acetic acid (HI–AcOH) (1.10) [17], hydrothermal reduction (0.90) [25], and hydrazine hydrate (1.63) [26]. This implies that our synthetic route is more effective in producing high quality graphene than other chemical processes.

From HRTEM analysis, the ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell structure (Fig. 3 (a)) is clearly evident. The outer graphene layers over the ZnO core are shown with red arrows. The close inspection of the dark areas in one of the images (Fig. 3(b)) taken from the layer encircling ZnO QDs reveals that the layer is a monolayer graphene because a hexagonal atomic lattice with uniform contrast can be clearly discerned, and the measured distance between carbon atoms is of about 0.14nm (inset of Fig. 3(b)). Another magnified view (Fig. 3(c)) clearly shows the curved graphene layers, shown as blue dot covered regions. Because corrugation and scrolling are part of the intrinsic nature of graphene due to the 2D membrane structure becoming thermodynamically stable through bending[11], graphene sheets with a few layers (monolayer, bilayer, trilayer, quadlayer, and pentalayer) from one edge with isolated fragments are clearly observed in the HRTEM image of the exfoliated graphene from the ZnO–graphene QDs (Fig. 3(d)).

We also carried out detailed XPS analysis in order to estimate the C and O contents and relative C/O ratio (XPS) for GO with and without KMnO4 including ZnO–graphene QD. The chemical composition of graphene layers after removal of ZnO component in ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs is described as well through the atomic ratio of carbon and oxygen (C/O) obtained by taking the ratio of C1s to O1s peak areas in XPS spectra. (see also Supplementary information, Figs. S3 and S5).

The main features of an EELS spectrum (Fig. 4 (b)) of the carbon K-edge region of the synthesized graphene layers in the small circle (denoted as 1 in the HAADF STEM images of Fig. 4(a)) are a peak at 284.9eV corresponding to transition from the 1s to the π∗ states (1s–π∗) and a peak at 292.7eV corresponding to transitions from the 1s to the σ∗ states (1s–σ∗) and these energy positions are coincident with those measured in graphene single-layer using the STEM [19,27]. Fig. 4(d) shows the EELS spectra taken from the small red circle areas of Fig. 4(c), denoted as points 1–4, at a low acceleration voltage of 80kV. The plasmon spectrum taken in a clean patch of one layer graphene exhibits the π-mode at 4.53eV and a π+σ-surface mode at 14.4eV, which are substantially red-shifted from their values of 4.96 and 26eV in graphite, respectively. Compared to the theoretical spectra, these two modes are well assigned to the π and π+σ in-plane modes of graphene at 4.8 and 14.5eV [28]. The π plasmon peak weakly increases in magnitude and position, whereas the π+σ plasmon gradually moves to higher energies and is accompanied by further broadening. The integrated intensity under the curve (between 10 and 35eV) increases to about 3.1, 5.9, and 10.0 times the value of monolayer graphene, and they can be assigned to the graphene films consisting of N =3, 6, and 10 layers, respectively [28]. The π+σ plasmon (N =6) evolves through development of a plateau between 15 and 20eV, and that (N =10) shows the peak maximum at 22eV, which can be ascribed to the increasing involvement of the bulk plasmon (25eV) mode of graphite. The other evidences of the graphene and ZnO–graphene core–shell QDs were presented in Supplementary S4 and S6.

We have demonstrated that the proposed approach to the synthesis of graphene layers through chemical reaction between GO with zinc acetate and a subsequent process of a substantial removal of ZnO core from ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs avoids the use of harmful chemicals and enables expeditious production of graphene layers in gram-scale quantities. From the D, G, and 2D band regions of the Raman spectrum of ZnO–graphene quasi core–shell QDs and the synthesized graphene layers, the ratio of the I D/I G (=0.1) of graphene is much lower than any values ever reported in previous chemical methods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors, Dong Ick Son and Won Kook Choi would like to appreciate the financial support from KIST Future Resource Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.03.021.
